metadata.title: Why a number's color in a financial model tells you whether you're allowed to touch it
metadata.purpose: The industry color standard makes each cell's SOURCE visible: blue = hardcoded input, black = formula, green = link within the workbook, red = external-file link. Color turns an invisible dependency graph into something the eye reads before touching anything.
metadata.clock: narration (Kokoro af_kore) — master clock; conform each beat to measured audio
metadata.still_lanes: raster (cell grid, color fills), c2v (dependency arrows)
metadata.exclusions: number-format codes, the recalc engine, charting, the full model-audit workflow
metadata.visual_object: A single projection column whose cells recolor one by one as provenance is revealed — blue inputs, black formulas, green cross-sheet links, red external links.
metadata.hook: Two cells both display "1,260," but one is a typed assumption you're meant to change and the other is a formula you must never overwrite — and only the color of the digits distinguishes them.

beats.narration_text: A revenue projection holds a blue 1,000 (input), a black 1,050 (=units×(1+growth)), a green cell pulled from another tab, and a red cell linked to an external file. Four identical-looking numbers, four different meanings.
beats.scaffold_note: from Key case — cold open on the concrete instance

beats.narration_text: A cell's meaning should come from what it computes; this convention carries meaning in font color instead — why encode provenance in color rather than in the formula?
Two cells both display "1,260," but one is a typed assumption you're meant to change and the other is a formula you must never overwrite — and only the color of the digits distinguishes them.

The industry color standard makes each cell's SOURCE visible: blue = hardcoded input, black = formula, green = link within the workbook, red = external-file link. Color turns an invisible dependency graph into something the eye reads before touching anything.
beats.visual: A single projection column whose cells recolor one by one as provenance is revealed — blue inputs, black formulas, green cross-sheet links, red external links.
beats.scaffold_note: the resolving mechanism; orbits the visual object

beats.narration_text: Units 1,000 (blue), growth 5% (blue). Year 1 = units×price → black. Year 2 = Year1×(1+growth) → black. COGS from a "Costs" tab → green. Benchmark from `market.xlsx` → red. Analyst edits only blue; every black cell recomputes.

The industry color standard makes each cell's SOURCE visible: blue = hardcoded input, black = formula, green = link within the workbook, red = external-file link. Color turns an invisible dependency graph into something the eye reads before touching anything.
